skippedSources.name: Haymaker (haymaker@substack.com)
skippedSources.reason: Paywall teaser repost of a Founding-Member alert — only an intro sentence, zero actual alert content in the free email.

items.title: Fed-hike odds swung from 61% to 54% in a week — and gold felt every point of it
items.idea: Oil spiked ~$8/bbl on Iran tensions then gave back ~$5, dragging 2026 Fed-hike odds from a midweek peak of 61% down to roughly 54%. Gold is holding the $3,900–$4,100 support built by the Oct/Nov 2025 lows; a break above $4,300–$4,600 would confirm the correction is over. Silver held $45–$55 support and is now retesting $60–$70 resistance.
items.angle: Gold's entire near-term trading range is now a direct read on oil-driven Fed-hike probability — the metal doesn't need its own catalyst, it needs a Hormuz outcome.
items.tweetA: Gold's trading range right now is basically a barometer for oil-driven Fed hike odds — 61% to 54% in a week, and the metal moved in lockstep. You don't need a gold view, you need an Iran view.
items.tweetB: $8 up, $5 back down. That's how sensitive Fed-hike pricing has become to one week of Gulf headlines. Whatever breaks the Hormuz standoff, either way, is the real gold catalyst — not Tuesday's CPI print.

charts.desc: Oil 5-day price chart plus gold/silver/platinum support-resistance zone charts.

items.title: Second US bank failure of 2026 — Kentland Federal, first since May 1
items.idea: The OCC closed Kentland Federal Savings and Loan of Indiana; the FDIC brokered a sale of its assets and deposits to the unrelated, similarly-named Kentland Bank. It's the first US bank failure since May 1 — a gap of over two months.
items.angle: One failure isn't a trend, but it's landing right as Warsh signals higher-for-longer and real yields grind higher — exactly the environment where small-bank credit stress gets dismissed until it isn't.
items.tweetA: A tiny Indiana thrift just became 2026's 2nd bank failure. Nobody notices until the 5th one. Real 5s and 10s yields above 2% aren't free — someone's balance sheet is eating that repricing right now.
items.tweetB: First bank failure since May 1, right as rate hikes are back on the table. If you're long regional bank credit on the soft-landing story, this is the kind of headline that's easy to shrug off alone and expensive to ignore in aggregate.

items.title: The curve math if the Fed delivers one more 25bp hike
items.idea: BondBeat's breakdown of a final 25bp hike: 2yr +15-25bp, 5yr +10-20bp, 10yr +5-15bp, 30yr flat to +10bp — a curve that flattens on the hike itself because the long end already trades fiscal deficits and Treasury supply, not just Fed policy.
items.angle: The front end has the most room to reprice hawkish, which means a 2s10s flattener is a bet on Warsh actually delivering rather than just talking tough — an underpriced asymmetry.
items.tweetA: If Warsh hikes 25bp: 2yr +15-25bp, 30yr flat to +10bp. That's not a parallel shift, that's the front end doing almost all the work. The flattener trade is a bet on follow-through, not on the number itself.
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether the Fed hikes. Nobody's pricing that the 30-year barely moves either way because it's already trading fiscal supply, not Fed policy. Two different variables driving one curve.

items.title: Warsh's opacity strategy might be a stealth inflation fighter — via money velocity
items.idea: Enduring Investments argues eliminating Fed forward guidance raises policy uncertainty, which raises demand for precautionary cash balances, which lowers money velocity — and lower velocity means a lower price level for the same money supply. They say Economic Policy Uncertainty is statistically significant (1% level) in their velocity model.
items.angle: This reframes 'less Fed communication' from a transparency complaint into an actual disinflationary channel — the opposite of how most people are reading Warsh's silence.
items.tweetA: Everyone reads Fed opacity as 'less info, more volatility.' Enduring Investments flips it: opacity to precautionary cash hoarding to lower velocity to lower prices. Warsh's silence might be a stealth tightening tool nobody's pricing.
items.tweetB: Same mechanism explains why 2020-21 stimulus didn't blow up inflation immediately — people sat on the cash out of fear before spending it. If uncertainty is now the policy, expect the inflation lag to work the same way in reverse.

items.title: $20B in rate-hedge flow built in six weeks — and it might not be a volatility trade at all
items.idea: Morgan Stanley: crude, Fed pricing, and 10yr yields moved back into lockstep after the relationship had broken down. Estimated 10y-equivalent 6% payer exposure hit $20B in six weeks — MS thinks this reflects construction-lending hedging, not a vol trade. A 200bp selloff could mechanically force sales near half the current duration exposure.
items.angle: If this is hedging demand rather than speculation, the flow is sticky and won't unwind on a rally — but the mechanical dealer convexity in a tail selloff is real and underappreciated.
items.tweetA: $20B of 6% payer exposure built in 6 weeks, and Morgan Stanley thinks it's construction-loan hedgers, not vol traders. That matters: hedging flow doesn't quit when rates dip, but it does turn into a forced seller if yields blow through 6%.
items.tweetB: Oil, Fed pricing, and 10yr yields just re-synced into lockstep after months of decoupling. When three variables that 'shouldn't' move together start moving together again, the market has usually found its one dominant narrative for the next leg.

charts.desc: Brent crude vs 10yr UST yield overlay, July 6-10.

items.title: The scary labor-force-participation decline is mostly a data artifact, not a real exodus
items.idea: Morgan Stanley: the labor force participation rate's -0.8pp year-over-year drop looks alarming, but 0.4pp of that is just delayed incorporation of new demographic estimates. Corrected, LFPR was roughly flat through May (-0.1pp vs the reported -0.5pp). The further June drop is expected to reverse.
items.angle: Anyone trading a 'labor market is cracking' narrative off the headline LFPR number is trading a statistical revision artifact, not an actual behavioral shift.
items.tweetA: Half the 'workers are leaving the labor force' story this year is a demographic reclassification, not actual behavior. Real LFPR change through May: -0.1pp, not -0.5pp. Check your data source before you build a recession thesis on it.
items.tweetB: Good reminder that 'the number' and 'the number after methodology changes' can tell opposite stories. If your labor-market bear case leans on LFPR, you're arguing with a spreadsheet update, not the economy.

charts.desc: LFPR chart, June 2025-May 2026, corrected for demographic-estimate distortion.

items.source: The Bond Beat / market breadth note
items.title: Market breadth is the quietest it's been in years — and quiet usually means a top, not calm
items.idea: The NYSE McClellan A-D Oscillator is hovering near zero, the lowest 15-day range reading in years. Combined with a low VIX and pinching Bollinger Bands, this is the classic signature of a price top, not a bottom — the Fosback Absolute Breadth Indicator flagged the same thing the prior week. Notably, gold's volatility signature is inverted: quiet rounded bottoms, violent spiky tops.
items.angle: Low realized vol plus low breadth dispersion is being read as 'safe' by most, but this specific combination has historically coincided with tops, not continuations.
items.tweetA: Quietest breadth reading in years on the NYSE right now — low VIX, pinching Bollinger Bands, near-zero McClellan Oscillator. Every prior instance of this exact combo marked a top. Quiet markets don't do that at bottoms.
items.tweetB: Funny detail: gold does the opposite of stocks. Stocks go quiet at tops and violent at bottoms; gold goes quiet at bottoms and violent at tops. Want to know which regime you're in? Check which asset is making the noise.

charts.desc: McClellan Oscillator 15-day range chart. 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Leveraged funds added 52.8k contracts to their 30-year short in a single week
items.idea: CFTC positioning data via Hedgopia: the 30-year Treasury bond futures net short position is now 143.6k contracts, up 52.8k week-over-week — a 37% jump in bearish long-bond positioning in one print.
items.angle: A 37% jump in net shorts in a week is crowding, not conviction — exactly the one-sided positioning that sets up a violent short squeeze if CPI surprises soft.
items.tweetA: 30yr net shorts jumped 52.8k contracts in a week to 143.6k — a 37% increase in one print. When positioning moves that fast in one direction, squeeze risk on a soft CPI is bigger than the base case being priced.
items.tweetB: Everyone's bearish long bonds into Warsh's testimony. The crowding itself is the risk now — the trade doesn't need to be wrong, it just needs one soft data point to unwind 50k contracts built in a week.

charts.desc: No chart in email; CoT figures cited narratively.

items.title: $743 billion of Treasury notes and bonds sold in a single week
items.idea: US Treasury debt outstanding (notes+bonds) grew $70B in one week on $743B of gross issuance. WolfStreet frames the 30yr's 5.06% yield as the bond market having 'completely blown off' the Fed's 2024-25 rate cuts, pricing inflation risk, a lax Fed, and heavy ongoing supply instead.
items.angle: $743B in a week is a supply shock hiding in plain sight every week — the real question is whether primary dealers can keep absorbing this pace without concessions widening.
items.tweetA: $743 billion of Treasuries sold in ONE week. The 30yr at 5.06% isn't pricing Fed policy anymore — it's pricing who's left to buy this much paper. Supply is now a bigger variable than the dot plot.
items.tweetB: The bond market shredded Powell's rate cuts — yields never followed the Fed down in 2024-25 and aren't following it now. When the market ignores the policy rate for two years running, stop calling it 'the Fed put' and start calling it 'the deficit tax.'

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (thedarksideoftheboom)
items.title: Markets now price 75% odds of a September hike — and almost zero cuts through 2028
items.idea: Fed funds futures imply ~75% probability of a 25bp hike by September and ~34% odds of another by year-end, both up over the week. More strikingly, the market sees little realistic chance of any rate cut through the end of 2028. Real 10yr TIPS yields are up ~30bp over the past year while breakevens have fallen ~11bp — a real-rate-driven tightening, not an inflation-expectations one.
items.angle: 'No cuts through 2028' repriced into the curve is a bigger deal than the next hike itself — the market has stopped pricing any mean-reversion in policy for over two years.
items.tweetA: The market isn't just pricing a hike. It's pricing NO CUTS through 2028. That's the real headline — a multi-year regime call, not a single-meeting call. Every leveraged-growth trade priced on 'eventually rates come down' just got a much longer runway to be wrong.
items.tweetB: Real 10yr yields up 30bp in a year, breakevens down 11bp. That's not an inflation scare — that's the market demanding more compensation for duration risk itself, separate from inflation. Two different taxes on every long-duration asset, and only one shows up in the CPI print.

charts.desc: No chart in email; figures cited narratively from Fed funds futures.

items.title: OpenAI ships 3 durable model tiers instead of one version number
items.idea: GPT-5.6 launches as three tiers — Sol (flagship, $5/$30 per million input/output tokens), Terra (matches 5.5 at lower cost, $2.50/$15), Luna (fastest/cheapest, $1/$6) — with tiers advancing independently of the generation number, and it's now the default brain behind Codex and ChatGPT Work agents.
items.angle: Decoupling tier name from version number is OpenAI admitting 'one model to rule all use cases' is dead — the real competition is cost-per-agent-task, not benchmark leaderboards.
items.tweetA: OpenAI just decoupled tier names (Sol/Terra/Luna) from version numbers. That's a tell: they no longer believe in one model line for everything. The AI race is quietly becoming a race on cost-per-agent-task, not raw capability.
items.tweetB: Luna at $1/$6 per million tokens as the 'default' agent brain tells you where the actual margin pressure is in this industry — not at the frontier, but in the cheap tier running every routine agent loop, millions of times a day.

items.title: Meta's new model beats Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 on agentic benchmarks — and opens its API for the first time
items.idea: Muse Spark 1.1 posts JobBench 54.7 vs Opus 4.8's 48.4 and GPT-5.5's 38.3, and 62.1 vs 57.9 on Humanity's Last Exam with tools. It supports a 1M-token context window, and Meta opened its Model API to developers for the first time, priced at $1.25/$4.25 per million tokens with $20 free credits.
items.angle: Meta going from 'no API business' to undercutting OpenAI's cheapest agent tier on day one is more interesting than the benchmark win itself.
items.tweetA: Meta just beat Opus 4.8 by 6 points and GPT-5.5 by 16 points on JobBench — and priced its API cheaper than OpenAI's cheapest tier on debut. That's not a research flex, that's a market-share opening move.
items.tweetB: First time Meta opens a paid model API, and they lead with an agentic benchmark win over the incumbents. Compare that to Llama's open-weights-only strategy — Meta has decided it wants a piece of the agent infrastructure business, not just mindshare.

items.title: a16z's pitch for the 'boring' crypto asset class nobody's trading
items.idea: Arcade tokens — stable-value, non-speculative tokens like Blackbird's $FLY loyalty token, usable across a network of competing restaurants — are pitched as the most underrated token type precisely because they're not investable. Faucet (mint) and sink (redemption) mechanics cap price at both ends, and interoperability turns competitors (a local coffee shop and Starbucks) into 'coopetition' partners sharing a loyalty pool.
items.angle: The entire crypto narrative is speculation-driven, so a16z pushing the least-speculative token type is either a genuine second-order bet on real-world payment rails, or a way to make regulators comfortable with tokenization by leading with the least scary example.
items.tweetA: a16z is out here evangelizing the one crypto asset class explicitly designed to NOT go up in value. That's either the smartest long-game regulatory play in crypto right now, or an admission that speculative tokens have run out of story.
items.tweetB: Starbucks and your local coffee shop sharing a loyalty token sounds insane until you realize airlines have run 'coopetition' via mileage partnerships for 30 years — just on a closed, non-portable ledger. The onchain version is the same business model with the moat removed.

charts.desc: No embedded charts; diagrams likely in the full post.

items.title: McKinsey's four trends set to redefine consumer behavior in 2026
items.idea: McKinsey flags four converging shifts: a new tech-driven path to purchase, a 'health revolution' in spending priorities, the experience economy, and the rise of the 'resourceful consumer' — someone actively routing around cost pressure rather than simply cutting back.
items.angle: 'Resourceful consumer' implies spend is being rerouted (resale, private label, deal-stacking), not destroyed — a very different signal for margins than a simple demand-pullback thesis.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's framing of 2026 consumers as 'resourceful' rather than 'retreating' matters — it implies spend is being rerouted (resale, private label, deal stacking), not destroyed. Different read for staples margins than a demand-destruction thesis.
items.tweetB: Four trends, one throughline: none of them are 'consumer pulls back.' Tech-driven purchase paths, health spending, experiences, resourcefulness — this is a consumer finding new channels to keep spending, not retrenching. Check that against your recession-in-retail thesis.

items.title: McKinsey's playbook: 5 instruments for building geopolitical foresight into strategy
items.idea: McKinsey argues strategic foresight isn't about predicting the future but building the capability to navigate multiple potential futures, laying out five specific instruments for doing so.
items.angle: A management consultancy productizing 'geopolitical scenario planning' as a repeatable corporate function shows how mainstream tail-risk planning has become in C-suites since 2022.
items.tweetA: Geopolitical scenario planning going from 'thing hedge funds do' to a packaged 5-step McKinsey framework for every corporate strategy team is itself a data point — tail-risk planning just became a standard line item, not a specialty.
items.tweetB: If McKinsey is selling geopolitical foresight as a repeatable process to every Fortune 500 client, that's the market telling you 2022-2026 wasn't a blip — corporates are now permanently budgeting for the world being unstable.

items.title: McKinsey names the next org chart: the 'symbiotic enterprise'
items.idea: McKinsey argues advances in cognitive and physical AI are ushering in a new organizational model built around human-AI collaboration, outlining four conditions required for it to succeed.
items.angle: This is McKinsey's tell that org-design consulting revenue is about to be reshaped by AI — the product isn't 'use AI tools,' it's 'redesign the org chart around AI as a coworker,' a much bigger, stickier engagement.
items.tweetA: McKinsey isn't pitching 'add AI tools' anymore — it's pitching redesigning the entire org chart around human-AI collaboration. That's the tell that consulting revenue is shifting from software-rollout fees to structural-reorg fees.
items.tweetB: 'Symbiotic enterprise' is corporate-speak for: your management layers were built for humans coordinating humans, and that architecture doesn't work when half your workforce is an agent. Nobody's really solved this yet — that's the actual opportunity.

items.title: Robots are leaving the factory floor for 'varied, everyday settings'
items.idea: McKinsey frames general-purpose robot adoption as shifting the core challenge from 'what can the technology do' to 'how do people and machines work together' as robots move beyond warehouses into everyday settings.
items.angle: This mirrors exactly where LLM agents were 18 months ago — the hard problem moving from capability to workflow-integration is the standard inflection signal for a technology about to scale.
items.tweetA: McKinsey says the robotics bottleneck just flipped from 'capability' to 'workflow integration.' That's the exact same inflection point LLM agents hit about 18 months before enterprise adoption took off. If the pattern holds, general-purpose robotics timelines just compressed.
items.tweetB: Nobody talks about robots in warehouses anymore — the interesting frontier is 'everyday settings.' That's a tell the unit economics finally work outside controlled, high-value industrial environments, which is the actual scaling unlock.

items.title: Geopolitics topped investor concerns before the Middle East conflict even started
items.idea: McKinsey's investor survey found geopolitics was already the foremost concern for investors in 2026 even before the current Middle East conflict escalated, with AI disruption and capital allocation discipline also top of mind.
items.angle: If geopolitics was already the #1 concern pre-escalation, the current Gulf tension isn't creating a new worry, it's confirming one investors already had — meaning positioning was probably already defensive, not caught offside.
items.tweetA: Geopolitics was already investors' #1 worry BEFORE the current Gulf escalation. That changes the read on this conflict: it's confirming a fear already priced, not shocking positioning that wasn't. Different market reaction function than a true tail event.
items.tweetB: Capital allocation discipline sitting right next to geopolitics and AI disruption as a top-3 investor concern in 2026 is the quiet story — after years of 'growth at any cost' AI capex, investors are starting to ask for the receipts.

items.title: AXT Technology doubled, then fully round-tripped — a live case study in momentum decay
items.idea: AXT Technology ($AXTI) nearly doubled in weeks after Herb Greenberg first flagged it in March, then fully round-tripped and more. Oklo ($OKLO) is well off its high-flying peak. Modine Manufacturing ($MOD), tied to the data-center narrative, is now living the 'live-by, die-by' side of that trade.
items.angle: Three separate 'hot theme' names (rare-earth/semis, nuclear SMR, data-center cooling) all round-tripping in the same window is a pattern, not three coincidences — speculative froth adjacent to AI is deflating faster than AI itself.
items.tweetA: $AXTI doubled then gave it all back. $OKLO round-tripped. $MOD is now on the 'die-by' side of the data-center trade. Three unrelated tickers, same pattern, same few weeks. That's not noise, that's the speculative layer around AI infra deflating first.
items.tweetB: The AI trade itself hasn't cracked. The stuff levered to the AI trade three degrees removed — rare earth suppliers, SMR hopefuls, data-center cooling plays — is round-tripping hard. Watch the picks-and-shovels-of-the-picks-and-shovels names for the real froth signal.

charts.desc: No chart in email; ticker moves referenced narratively.

items.title: GFL Environmental rumor resurfaced over July 4th, stock spiked then settled back
items.idea: A rumor about GFL Environmental ($GFL) floated over the July 4 holiday, drove more stories the following Monday, and pushed the stock higher before it settled back — Greenberg has flagged the name before as a 'dysfunction junction' situation.
items.angle: Holiday-week rumor spikes that fully mean-revert within days are a decent tell for how much of the move was thin-liquidity positioning versus real information.
items.tweetA: Holiday-week rumor, stock pops, then fully settles back by the following week. $GFL is turning into a textbook case of low-liquidity headline trading — fade July 4th-week spikes on illiquid names until volume actually confirms.
items.tweetB: Rumors that need a three-day weekend to catch fire and a full week to fade usually tell you more about market structure (nobody's around to fade it) than about the company. Worth remembering for any August/holiday-week headline.

items.title: Edwards Lifesciences: new turnaround CFO plus positive percutaneous TAVR data for moderate valve disease
items.idea: Edwards ($EW) hired Doretta Mistras as CFO, a former healthcare investment banker who helped turn around Vistra ($VST), alongside positive clinical trial results for using percutaneous (non-open-heart) TAVR to treat moderate aortic valve disease — expanding the addressable population beyond severe cases.
items.angle: Moving TAVR into moderate-severity patients is a much bigger TAM expansion than the CFO hire — severe aortic stenosis is a fraction of the moderate-disease population currently just monitored with annual echocardiograms.
items.tweetA: The CFO hire is the small story. The real one: positive trial data for treating MODERATE aortic valve disease with percutaneous TAVR. Severe cases are a fraction of the moderate population currently just getting watched every year. That's the TAM expansion.
items.tweetB: A device company hiring a CFO who turned around a power company ($VST) is a specific signal — that's a 'we need someone who's fixed a balance sheet under pressure' hire, not a routine succession.

items.title: A 200+ name database of AI operator-angels just got published
items.idea: A new database lists 200+ early-stage US investors who have hands-on experience building AI products and companies, with background, current role, firm, and direct contact details for founder outreach.
items.angle: The existence of a curated 'operator-angel' list specifically for AI is itself a sign the AI angel pool has gotten big enough to need indexing — a maturity signal for the ecosystem, not just a networking tool.
items.tweetA: There's now a 200+ name database specifically for AI operator-angels. When a sub-category of angel investor gets big enough to need its own directory, that sub-category has officially matured into an asset class.
items.tweetB: Worth cross-referencing against your cap table next raise — 'operator who built AI before' is becoming the single most valuable line on an angel's bio, more than fund size or brand name.

items.title: Teams are replacing one-off prompting with autonomous loops — shipping migrations in days, not months
items.idea: Developers are shifting from single prompts to automated loops (turn-based, goal-based, time-based, proactive) that plan, execute, and refine work with minimal input — reportedly compressing large code migrations from months to days.
items.angle: This is exactly the workflow-orchestration pattern becoming standard practice across the industry, not just a power-user technique.
items.tweetA: 'Months to days' on large code migrations by switching from prompting to autonomous loops. If that ratio holds even at half strength across a codebase, the constraint on software throughput stops being engineer-hours and starts being how well you can specify the loop.
items.tweetB: The prompt-to-loop shift is the same evolution every automation technology goes through: manual operation to scripted macro to autonomous agent. Code migration is just the first place it's showing up with a hard, measurable before/after number.

items.title: AI companies are betting $10B on embedding engineers inside customer orgs
items.idea: Major AI companies are investing billions in 'forward deployed engineers' who work directly inside customer organizations, strengthening long-term relationships, reducing switching, and surfacing product insights software alone can't generate.
items.angle: This is the enterprise-AI version of Palantir's forward-deployed-engineer moat — expensive, doesn't scale like software, but it's the actual switching-cost mechanism, more durable than any single feature.
items.tweetA: $10B on embedding engineers inside customer companies is AI vendors admitting software alone doesn't create lock-in — the relationship and integration work does. That's Palantir's playbook, and now the whole industry is copying the moat, not just the product.
items.tweetB: Forward-deployed engineering doesn't scale like SaaS margins — it's people, not code. The AI companies making this bet are explicitly trading near-term gross margin for switching costs. Worth checking which public AI-adjacent names actually disclose this headcount.

items.title: Top-tier seed valuations: $25.8M in 2017 to $200M+ in 2026
items.idea: The top tier of seed-stage startups has climbed from $25.8M valuations in 2017 to more than $200M in 2026 — nearly 8x — with the fastest growth concentrated at the very top, widening the gap between standout companies and everyone else.
items.angle: An 8x increase in top-decile seed valuations over 9 years, concentrated at the extreme top, mirrors the same 'top decile of outcomes' concentration showing up in public equities and mega-cap tech — the barbell is everywhere right now.
items.tweetA: Top seed valuations: $25.8M in 2017, $200M+ now. That's not seed-stage anymore, that's Series B pricing at the idea stage — and it's ONLY happening at the top decile. The gap between the best deal in a batch and everything else has never been wider.
items.tweetB: Same barbell showing up in venture that's showing up in public markets: extreme concentration at the top, nothing in the middle. If seed valuations are following the same power-law-on-steroids pattern as mega-cap tech weight in the S&P, that's a market-structure story, not a sector story.

charts.desc: No chart in email; figure cited from Peter Walker.

items.title: France offers the world's most generous vacation policy — and workers still don't use it all
items.idea: France has the most generous vacation allotment globally, yet most French workers leave part of it unused, with usage patterns varying widely across countries — showing time-off behavior depends as much on culture as on policy.
items.angle: This directly undercuts the standard 'just mandate more PTO' policy prescription — if the country with the most generous policy still under-uses it, the constraint on rest is cultural/managerial, not legislative.
items.tweetA: France has the best vacation policy on earth and workers still leave days on the table. If policy alone solved burnout, this wouldn't happen. The constraint isn't days-allotted, it's manager culture and guilt — no legislation fixes that.
items.tweetB: Worth remembering next time a US company touts 'unlimited PTO' as a perk — France proves generous policy without a culture that actually uses it just becomes a number nobody redeems. Days-on-paper and days-actually-taken are two different metrics.

items.title: Researchers report the first AI-agent-led ransomware attack — credential discovery and ransom note, fully automated
items.idea: Security researchers report an AI system reportedly handled credential discovery and generated its own ransom note in what's described as the first AI-agent-led ransomware attack, with security teams warning automation could make sophisticated attacks easier to run at scale.
items.angle: The 'first' label matters less than the specific detail — the agent generated its OWN ransom note, meaning the attack chain from recon to extortion messaging needed zero human authorship, which is the actual scaling unlock for attackers.
items.tweetA: The scary detail isn't 'AI helped with ransomware' — it's that the agent wrote its OWN ransom note. Recon, credential discovery, and extortion messaging with zero human authorship in the loop. That's the automation unlock security teams should actually be modeling for.
items.tweetB: Every AI safety conversation about agents has been hypothetical until this. If credential discovery plus ransom-note generation is now a fully agentic chain, the marginal cost of running a ransomware campaign just dropped toward zero. That's a supply-side shock to attack volume, not just a new technique.

items.title: SambaNova raises $1B at an $11B valuation with a major banking partnership
items.idea: SambaNova closed a $1B raise at an $11B valuation, strengthening its position in enterprise AI infrastructure, alongside a major banking partnership signaling rising demand for private, on-premises AI deployments.
items.angle: A banking partnership specifically (not a generic enterprise logo) is the tell — regulated industries want on-prem/private inference precisely because they can't send data to a shared cloud API, a structurally different market than the hyperscaler AI story.
items.tweetA: $1B at $11B for SambaNova, anchored by a bank partnership. That's not competing with OpenAI on capability — that's competing on 'your data never leaves your building,' which is a completely different sales motion and moat.
items.tweetB: Every dollar into on-prem AI infra like SambaNova is a bet that regulated industries (banking, healthcare, defense) will never be comfortable on shared hyperscaler clouds no matter how good the compliance story gets. Worth tracking whether that thesis strengthens or weakens in 2026.

items.title: This 4-year rally is running in the top decile of 100 years of market cycles — +80% vs a +30% median
items.idea: The current cycle is tracking the top decile of every 4-year market path over the past century, up roughly +80% versus a median cycle's +30% at the same point. The three prior tenants of that top-decile 'penthouse' — the run into 1929, the melt-up into 2000, the everything-rally into late 2021 — were each followed by drawdowns of -89% (Dow, 1932), -78% (Nasdaq, 2002), and -19.4% (S&P, 2022).
items.angle: The framing worth stealing: 'the higher above the median path you start, the thinner the reward the next 12 months pays' — a testable, falsifiable statement about right now, not a vague valuation worry.
items.tweetA: This cycle is +80% vs a 100-year median of +30% at the same point. The last three times we were in this specific top-decile seat, the drawdowns were -89%, -78%, and -19.4%. Extraordinary returns aren't a promise of more — they're historically the tripwire before the opposite.
items.tweetB: Nobody wants to hear 'we're in rarefied air' during a melt-up, but the math is simple: the further above trend you are, the less trend-following return you have left to harvest. This isn't a crash call, it's a compressed-forward-returns call from here — less dramatic, more useful.

charts.desc: 4-year cycle path percentile chart — current cycle vs historical median and top-decile paths. 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Only 135 of the S&P 500's 1996 constituents are still in the index — a 73% attrition rate
items.idea: Of the 500 companies in the S&P 500 in 1996, only 135 remain today, a -73% attrition rate. The Nifty Fifty — the 'one-decision stocks' investors were told to buy and hold forever — led the index down -48% into October 1974 when that cohort cracked.
items.angle: This is the actual rebuttal to 'just hold the index forever' complacency: the index survives by ruthlessly replacing losers, meaning today's mega-cap concentration is a feature investors are underpricing as a risk, not a permanent fixture.
items.tweetA: 73% of the S&P 500's 1996 lineup is gone — swallowed, spun off, delisted. The index doesn't compound, it CULLS. Which means today's 'unassailable' mega-cap winners are exactly the cohort history says to worry about, not the cohort to relax about.
items.tweetB: The Nifty Fifty were also 'obviously buy and hold forever' stocks. Then the S&P fell 48% into 1974 with the untouchables leading the way down. A book of unassailable winners has never correlated with safety — it correlates with concentration risk, a different thing entirely.

items.title: China's central bank just made its largest monthly gold purchase since October 2023 — into a selloff
items.idea: The PBOC bought 480,000 ounces of gold in June, its 20th consecutive month of accumulation and its largest since October 2023 — buying harder as gold pulled back to $4,077 (-2.37% on the session), not while it was ripping. Central banks bought 1,000+ tonnes/year through 2022-23 while gold ran from ~$1,600 to $4,000+, a 2.5x move; official-sector buying has historically led the multi-year trend, not trailed it.
items.angle: The PBOC accelerating purchases into weakness rather than strength is the opposite of retail/momentum behavior, and it's the single most price-insensitive buyer in the market — that combination has never coincided with a dying bull market historically.
items.tweetA: PBOC bought its largest monthly gold stack since Oct 2023 — in June, right into a pullback, not while gold was ripping. The most price-insensitive buyer on earth just accelerated into weakness. Every prior instance of that combo preceded the trend continuing, not ending.
items.tweetB: Gold's down $99 on the session to $4,077, blamed on 'higher for longer' and war-premium fears. Both are inherently temporary — a debt-soaked government can't sustain either indefinitely. Meanwhile the actual multi-year signal, official-sector buying, just hit a 2026 high. Which variable would you rather trade?
